pattern: Every node's full ancestor-plus-descendant set must be exactly the neighbors it has in the pair list, and an ancestor's such set always contains everything a descendant's set contains (minus their mutual pair); processing nodes from the largest such set down to the smallest, and matching each to the smallest still-bigger set that contains it, either reconstructs a unique tree, proves several are possible whenever two matched sets tie in size, or proves none exist when containment ever breaks.
hints: Build, for every value that appears in pairs, the set of every other value it is paired with -- that set is exactly its combined ancestors and descendants in any valid tree.
The one node whose set has size (total node count - 1) must be the root if a valid tree exists at all; if no node's set is that large, no tree exists.

Process the remaining nodes from the largest set down to the smallest; each one's parent must be the neighbor with the smallest set size that is still at least as large as its own, and everything else in its set (besides that parent) must also appear in the parent's set -- if that ever fails there is no valid tree, and if a node's chosen parent has a set of exactly the same size, more than one valid tree exists.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, ambiguous triangle of pairs
statement example, no valid tree exists
smallest possible input, a single pair
root is unambiguous but two middle nodes can swap depth with each other
no node has enough neighbors to be a root at all
unique star-shaped tree, one root with several independent leaves

stress.description: pairs derived from an actual random tree, so a valid reconstruction is always guaranteed to exist
stress.note: node labels are shuffled so the root is not always the smallest value; a chain shape can produce up to ~n^2/2 pairs, still comfortably under the length limit for n=30

complexity.time: O(n^2) worst case over the adjacency sets
